text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing adhesions from previous abdominal surgeries. Chokers are placed as indicated in the visual field.

The surgeon identifies the first portion of the duodenum and performs a retroduodenal dissection. ICG fluorescence is used to assess duodenal perfusion, followed by transection of the duodenum with a stapler. The gastroduodenal artery is visualized, and the marked jejunal loop is brought up in a supracolic fashion for anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an end-to-side posterior running suture layer using 3-0 PDS on the jejunum, which is transected three centimeters from the anastomosis with a stapler. A two-centimeter duodenotomy and enterotomy are created using a monopolar hook. The inner posterior running suture layer is completed with 3-0 PDS, followed by two anterior inner and outer running suture layers to finalize the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates two small enterotomies using a monopolar hook at the biliary limb and the second jejunal marking, located 130 centimeters from the ileocecal valve. An enteroenterostomy is performed using a stapler, and the enterostomies are closed in two uninterrupted running layers with 3-0 PDS sutures. An intraoperative EGD is conducted, showing no evidence of a leak.
